original_abstract: As part of one of the world's first truly global organizations, Jesuit missionaries played a pivotal role in the eighteenth-century exploration of the Sonoran Desert—a rugged territory spanning modern-day Sonora, Mexico, and Arizona, USA. Building on the foundation laid by the pioneer Padre Eusebio Kino, an increasing number of German-speaking missionaries arrived in the early 1700s to expand this mission network. Beyond their religious duties, these highly educated individuals served as early scientists and ethnographers, producing a vast corpus of written records that documented the geography, natural history, and indigenous cultures of this remote frontier. Although the Jesuits were expelled from the Americas in 1767 and temporarily suppressed on a global scale shortly thereafter, their legacy remains significant. Their detailed accounts—often preserved in monumental collections like Joseph Stöcklein’s *Welt-Bott*—stand as invaluable historical, anthropological, and scientific documents that offer a unique window into the complexities of early modern globalization and transcultural encounter.
humanized_abstract: When we think of globalization, many historical events and individuals come to mind. Sometimes, events unfold slowly over time—years, decades or even centuries—making it hard to grasp as a whole system or event. Another way to think about the events and individuals that act as agents of globalization is through looking at their influence on geography and societies around the world. For the Sonoran Desert, and regions beyond, a less familiar historical event might fit the bill as one of the world’s first truly global events. It had to do with faith and geography, but also features some familiar globalizing motifs and key actors, as I discovered while teaching in Tucson, Arizona last semester. To appreciate this aspect, you need to take a quick geography primer and understand a bit about globalizing agents of change: Starting several hundred years ago, Catholic missionaries —especially Jesuits—traveled to remote locations to do some very important faith work. Here, those who came to convert the local people ended up mapping deserts across the Americas from the Sinaloa area of northwestern Mexico, all the way to eastern California, Nevada, and parts of Arizona. Meanwhile, back in Europe, more missionaries emigrated, to build more missions for faith in the colonial era. A particularly important and influential one—Padreaching today’s southern Arizona and southwestern New Mexico—was founded by Padre Eusebio Kino who created detailed maps for the area. Missionaries from all over Germany would later follow a few years after him in the early 1700s to help spread the faith in the colonies across the continent to today’s Arizona. In these colonial areas far from the European metropole, an important duty beyond just proselytizing included gathering a lot of knowledge about the regions they traveled into. Each had unique skills as accomplished writers and scientists as well as ethnographers, collecting a huge collection of contemporary observations—information about not only local flora and fauna but also about the Native American peoples whose lands the missionary visitors were on for the first time in the colonial era. In this way, this network—already familiar to those learning about history of modern scientific inquiry—helped contribute to a general body of research—geographic and scientific studies of “foreign lands” or foreign places. Their major contributions lie within numerous texts of manuscripts that not only have comprehensive information of the lands that they visited across the far flung empire but include the “ethnography” of the indigenous people of those far flung outposts as well and were crucial to being an important part of the scientific and ethnographic knowledge created within that colonial system before they were expelled by the Spanish Crown in 1767 and eventually banned altogether two years later. Each of the major compendiums—such as the one used by scholars called *Welt-Bott* by Padre Stöcklein —are among those that create important insight into the Globalization processes that exist for thousands of years. This knowledge provides in-depth data, unique to this period of modern history for what constitutes the globalizing aspects of a long-distance connection system at work and constitutes a prime example of globalizing processes in the distant past and provide a general starting-point for further comparative studies about globalization over all of history.
